turns.text: Yes, those Russian students and intellectuals - I can recall that a doctor was also among them - were brought from the Moosburg Camp to Dachau' and after 1 month they were all executed. That was in March of 1944.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Do you happen to know what the reason was for their execution?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: The order for it came from Berlin. We did not get to know the reason, because I saw the bodies only after the execution and the reason was read aloud before the execution took Place.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: This execution produced the impression that it was one of the stages of the general plan for extermination of the people who entered Dachau?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. It was easy to see that these executions, these transports of invalids, and the way epidemics were dealt with, were all part of the general plan for extermination; and particularly, and this I must emphasize, it was the Russian prisoners who were always treated the worst of all.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Would you be so kind as to say what is known to you in regard to those internees who were in the " Nacht und Nebel " (night and fog) category? Were there many of these internees? Do you know the reason why they were sent to the concentration camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Many so-called Nacht und Nebel prisoners came to the concentration camp. The people so designated were mostly from the western countries of Europe, particularly Frenchmen, Belgians, and Dutchmen. The Russian people - and this was also the case with the Czechs and also in my own case - frequently had the designation "return undesirable." This actually meant the same. Shortly before the liberation many of these people were executed on the order of the camp commander, that is, shot in front of the crematory. Many of these people, particularly the French and Russians, were serious cases of typhus and with a temperature of 40 degrees were carried on stretchers to the rifle range.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: It seems to me that you mentioned something about a considerable number of prisoners who died of starvation. Could you tell me how large that number was - the number of people who died of starvation?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe that two-thirds of the entire population of the camp suffered from severe malnutrition and that at least 25 percent of the dead had literally died of starvation. It was called in German "Hungertyphus." Apart from that, tuberculosis was the most wide-spread disease in the camp and it spread also because of malnutrition. Most of its victims were Russians.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: It seems to me that you said, answering the question of my colleague, that the majority of those who died of starvation and exhaustion were French, Russians, and Italians. How do you account for the fact that in just these categories of internees more people died than in other categories?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: How do you explain that especially Russians, French, and Italians made up the largest number of those people who died from starvation? Was there any difference in the feeding of internees of the different nationalities, or was there some other reason?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: It was like this: The others, the Germans, Poles, and Czechs, who had already been in the camp for some time, had had time, if I may say so, to adjust themselves to camp conditions, physically I mean. The Russian deteriorated rapidly. The same was true of the French and the Italians. Moreover, these nationals for the most part arrived from other camps suffering from malnutrition so that they then soon fell easy prey to the other epidemics and diseases. Also, the Germans, Poles, and many others who worked in the armaments industry had since the year 1943 been able to get parcels from home. That, of course, was not the case with citizens of Soviet Russia, France, or Italy.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Can you answer the question about what Rosenberg, Kaltenbrunner, Sauckel, or Funk saw when they were in the Dachau Concentration Camp? Do you know what they saw and what was shown them?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I had no opportunity of seeing what happened during these visits. Only on very rare occasions did one have the opportunity of seeing these visitors from the window and observing where they went. I seldom had the opportunity to be present as I was in the case of Himmler's visits and those of Obergruppenfuehrer Pohl and once on the occasion of Gauleiter Giesler's visit, when they were shown the experiments or the patients in the hospital. As to the others I do not know what they individually saw and did in the camp.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Perhaps you had an opportunity of observing the length of the visit of those people in the camp, whether the visit was short just for a few moments - or whether they stayed there a long time. I have in mind Rosenberg, Kaltenbrunner, Sauckel, and Funk.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: That varied. Many visitors were there for half an hour, many, as I said before, spent as many as 3 hours there. We were always able to observe that quite well because at those times no work could be done, nor was food distributed. We did not carry on our work in the hospital and had to wait until the signal was given to us that the visitors had left the camp. Apart from that I had no means of knowing how long these visits in the camp lasted in the individual cases.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Can you recall the visit of Kaltenbrunner, Rosenberg, Funk, and Sauckel? On the basis of what you said just now could you state whether they were brief visits or whether those people stayed there for several hours? Did you understand my question or not?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Unfortunately, I cannot make a statement on that because, as I said, the visits took place so frequently that I have difficulty, after all these years, in recalling whether they lasted for a short or longer time. Many visits, for instance, from schools from the military and police schools - lasted a whole day.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Thank you. I have no further questions of this witness at this stage of the sitting. M. CHARLES DUBOST (Deputy Chief Prosecutor for the French Republic): You alluded to a convoy of deported French people who came from Compiegne, of whom only 1,200 survivors arrived. Were there any other convoys?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. There were transports, particularly from Bordeaux, Lyon, and Compiegne, all in the first half of 1944.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Were all the transports carried out under the same conditions?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: The conditions under which these transports were made were, if not the same, at any rate very similar.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Each time you were able to see on arrival that there were numerous victims?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: What were the causes of death?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: The deaths were caused by the fact that too many people were packed into the cars, which were then locked, and that they did not get anything to eat or drink for several days. Usually they starved or suffocated. Many of those who survived were brought to the camp hospital, and of these a large number died from various complications and diseases.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Did you make autopsies on the people who died while en route?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, particularly for the transport from Compiegne my services were demanded because the rumor was spread that the French Maquis and Fascists had attacked and killed each other in the cars. I had to inspect these corpses, but in no case did I find any signs of violence. Moreover, I took 10 corpses as a test, dissected them thoroughly and sent special reports on them to Berlin. All these people had died of suffocation. I was also able to note during the autopsy that these were prominent people of France. I could tell from their identity papers and uniforms that they were high-ranking French officers, priests, deputies, and well- nourished people who had been taken direct from civilian life to the cars and sent to Dachau.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: After the reports which you sent to Berlin did the conditions under which the transports were made remain the same?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Nothing happened, as usual. Always long reports were written but conditions did not improve at all.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: You indicated that some French generals had been put to death shortly before the liberation of the camp. Do you know the names of these generals?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Unfortunately I have forgotten these names I can remember only what I was told by the prisoners who were kept in the bunkers with them that they were the prominent personalities from Germany and other countries: Pastor Niemoller was there, also a French prince, Schuschnigg was there too, and members of the French Government and many others. I was told that one of the generals who had been shot was a close relative of General De Gaulle. Unfortunately I have forgotten his name.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: If I understood you correctly, these generals were prisoners of war who had been transported to this concentration camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: These two generals were not in the concentration camp. They were kept, along with the other prominent personalities, in the so-called "Kommandantur-Arrest," that is, in the bunker separated from the camp. On various occasions when they needed medical attention I came into contact with them, but that was very seldom. Otherwise they did not come into contact with the other prisoners at all.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Did they belong to the category of deported 'people whose "return was undesirable" or were they in the Nacht und Nebel category?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I do not know. It was 2 days previously that all the others who were kept in the bunker were sent by special transport to the Tyrol. That was, I believe, a week or 8 days before the liberation.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: You indicated that numerous visitors, German military men, students, political men, often toured the camp. Can you say if any ordinary people, like workers or farmers, knew what was going on in this camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: In my opinion, the people who lived in the neighborhood of Munich must have known of all these things, because the prisoners went every day to various factories in Munich and the neighborhood; and at work they frequently came into contact with the civilian workers. Moreover, the various suppliers and consumers often entered the fields and the factories of the German armament works and they saw what was done to the prisoners and what they looked like.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Can you say in what way the French were treated?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Well, if I said that the Russians were treated worst of all, the French were the second in order. Of course, there were differences in the treatment of individual persons. The Nacht und Nebel prisoners were treated quite differently; likewise the prominent political personalities and the intellectuals. That was so for all nationalities. And the workers and peasants also were treated differently.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: If I understood correctly, the treatment reserved for the French intellectuals was particularly rigorous. Do you remember the treatment inflicted on some French intellectuals and can you tell us their names?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I had many comrades among the physicians and university professors who worked with me in the hospital Unfortunately a large number of them died of typhus. Most of the French, in fact, died of typhus. I remember best of all Professor Limousin. He arrived in very poor condition with the transport from Compiegne. I took him into my department as assistant pathologist. Then I also knew the Bishop of Clermont-Ferrand. There were other physicians and university professors whom I knew. I remember Professor Roche, Dr. Lemartin, and many others I have forgotten their names.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: In the course of the conversations which you had with Dr. Rascher were you informed of the purpose of these experiments?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I didn't understand the question, excuse me please...

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Were you informed of the purpose of the medical and biological experiments made by Dr. Rascher in the camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Well, Dr. Rascher made exclusively so-called Air Force experiments in the camp. He was a major in the Air Force and was assigned to investigate the conditions to which parachutists were subjected and, secondly, the conditions of those people who had to make an emergency landing on the sea or had fallen into the sea. According to scientific standards, insofar as I can judge, this was all to no purpose. Like all the other experiments, it was simply useless murder; and it is amazing that learned university professors and physicians, particularly, were capable of carrying out these experiments according to plan. These experiments were much worse than all the liquidations and executions, because all the victims of these experiments simply had their suffering prolonged, as various medicines such as vitamins, hormones, tonics, and injections, which were not available for the ordinary patients, were provided for these patients so that the experiments might last longer and give those people more time to observe their victims.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: I am speaking now of the experiments of Dr. Rascher only. Had he received the order to make these experiments or did he make them on his own initiative?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: These experiments were made on Himmler's direct orders; also, Dr. Rascher had close relations with Himmler and was like a relative of his. He visited Himmler very often and Himmler visited Dr. Rascher several times.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Have you any information as to the kind of physicians who were making these experiments? Were they always SS men or were they members of medical faculties of universities who, however, did not belong to the SS?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: That varied. For example, the malaria station was under the direction of Professor Klaus Schilling of the Koch Institute in Berlin. The Phlegmone station also had several university professors. The surgical station was manned solely by SS doctors. In the Air Force station there were exclusively SS and military doctors. It differed. Dr. Bleibeck from Vienna conducted the experiments with sea water.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Were the experiments for the Luftwaffe made on the order of Himmler only?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Himmler.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Do you know - this is the last question - how many Frenchmen passed through this camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe at least eight or ten thousand people arrived at the camp. Furthermore, I know very well that, particularly during the last period, several thousand French prisoners marched on foot from the western camps, especially from Natzweiler, Struthof, et cetera, and that only very small remnants of these ever reached Dachau.

turns.speaker: M. DUBOST
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Can you tell us to what branches of the German service those who were employed at the camp belonged?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: If I understood you correctly, the highest authority on everything going on in the camp was the so-called Security Main Office in Berlin. All demands and directives came from Berlin; also the experimental stations received a definite quota of subjects for the experiments and the numbers were fixed by Berlin. If the doctors making the experiments needed a larger number, new requests had to be sent to Berlin.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, but what I want to know is to what branch of the service the men belonged who were employed in the camp.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: They were all SS men and most of them from the SD. During the last days, at the very end, a few members of the Armed Forces were there as guards but the men in charge were entirely SS men.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Were there any of the Gestapo there?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, that was the so-called political department, which was directed by the chief of the Munich Gestapo. It had control of all the interrogations and regulations, and it proposed the executions, transports, and transports of invalids. Also, all the people who were provided for the experiments had to be approved by the political department.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any of the defendants' counsel want to cross-examine the witness?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, you told us that at one time the Defendant Funk also was at Dachau, and you informed us, if I understood you correctly, that this happened on the occasion of some celebration or state conference between the Axis Powers. Please think back a little and tell us when that was approximately. Perhaps - just a moment perhaps you could tell us the year, maybe also the season, and perhaps you could also state which political celebration it was.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: As far as Funk is concerned, I can remember that it was, I believe, a conference of finance ministers. The papers had announced that it would take place and we were informed beforehand that some of the ministers would come to Dachau. Such a visit was actually made a few days afterwards, and it was said that Minister Funk was among the visitors. It was, I believe, during the first half of the year 1944. I cannot say that with absolute certainty.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You mean to say: during the first half of 1944, on the occasion of a conference of finance ministers?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. - DR. SAUTER: Where did that conference take place?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: If I remember correctly - I didn't write that down, of course - that was either in Salzburg or Reichenhall or Berchtesgaden, somewhere in the neighborhood of Munich, I believe.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: From whom did you learn at that time that on the next day, or the day after, high-ranking visitors would arrive?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: We always received an order to prepare for such a visit. Elaborate preparations were always made; everything was cleaned up; everything had to be in order, as you will understand; and those people whose presence might be undesirable or those who, in a certain sense, might be dangerous, had to disappear. Thus, whenever such high-ranking visitors were announced we always received an order from the camp headquarters 1 or 2 days beforehand; and, also these visitors were always accompanied by the camp commander.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: By the camp commander? Now, if you know that the Defendant Funk was there and people talked about it, then I think they would have mentioned also what other persons were present at this visit made by the Defendant Funk

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I cannot remember. There were always several important persons.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: The rest do not interest me. I am interested only in knowing whether or not at that particular visit, which was said to have been made by Funk, word was passed around the camp that such and such personalities were with him?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I cannot remember that now.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You cannot remember. Can you remember afterwards, perhaps on the next day or the day after, something was said perhaps by people who had seen the visitors?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, we always discussed that, but now I can no longer remember which personalities were mentioned.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, I am not interested in any other visit, but in this specific visit, as long as I do not say anything to the contrary. In this case I should like to know whether or not anything at all was said later on about the persons who were there with Funk.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: That I do not know; there were so many visits. For instance, after one visit, the very next day already another visit would be announced.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Now, you do also remember the visit that Funk made. Well, if other finance ministers were there, one would think that you would recall these other persons also.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I cannot remember that. It may be that the people with whom I talked did not know who these other persons were.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know why, or to put it differently, which departments of the camp were visited on the occasion when Funk was supposed to have made this visit. At any rate he did not come to you.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No; he did not come to the pathological department.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: He did not. But you were also prepared?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. All departments had always to be prepared, even if no visitors came. It also happened at times that a visit was announced, and then, for one reason or another, nothing came of it.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, as regards these observations of yours that you have related to us today, have you been interrogated in regard to them many times already?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I was interrogated on these matters for the first time before the military court at Dachau.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you also at that time say that Funk had been there? I repeat, did you before the military court at Dachau say anything to the effect that Funk had been present?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, I said the same thing before the court at Dachau.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: About Funk?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Also about Funk.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: But is it true, Witness? I ask again whether it is really true, because you are here as a witness under oath.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You were interrogated also the day before yesterday?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you, at that time, also make these statements about Funk?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I said the same thing at the interrogation conducted by the Prosecution.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Is that also in the record which I believe you signed?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I signed no record.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You signed no record?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No; I simply signed what was read by the Prosecution.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Well, that is a record.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, but in that record there is no mention of these visits.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Why then didn't you mention these visits the day before yesterday?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I was asked about it orally, and the prosecutor told me that these matters would be taken up orally in the courtroom.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Were you then also told where the defendants sit in the courtroom?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No. Before the military court I was shown all the pictures . . .

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Aha!

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: "And I was asked to identify to the court the various people. I identified the three of whom I said today that I had seen them in person. Funk and others I did not name.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You did not name Funk?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I did not say that I had personally seen him or that I could identify him.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: But when the pictures were shown to you did you see the defendants in the pictures?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Now, if I understand you correctly, you knew today where, for instance, Funk or Frick or anyone else was sitting?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Funk I do not know personally, because I did not see him at that time.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Were you not told when the pictures were shown to you at Dachau, "This is Funk; look at him; do you know him"?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No; that was done quite differently.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: How?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: All the pictures were shown to me and I was asked to say which of these individuals I had seen at the Dachau camp. Of these people I named these three. There was no further discussion whatsoever in regard to the other pictures.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Well, Dr. Blaha, when your hearing started and you were questioned by the President or by the prosecutor, you made a statement, I believe, in the Czech language.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: What then?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: In the German language.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: No; everyone heard that that was not German, but it was obviously Czech.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: The first sentence only.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: The first sentences? Well, now, as it will in any case come into the court transcript for practical purposes, I ask you to state and to repeat quite literally, giving the true sense, that which you sail then, because we are interested in that from the point of view of the Defense.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe that it was included in the transcript because an English translation was added to my statement.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: No, I do not believe that Czech is being translated. But anyhow please repeat it. We did not hear it.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. I said that I was ready, since it is technically impossible to use my native Czech tongue in the hearing, to give my testimony in German, because I have lived in German surroundings through all these events which occurred during the last 7 years and which are now the subject of this Trial. Moreover, the special and new expressions referring to life in the camp can be found only in German, and in no other dictionary can one find such suitable and expressive terms as in the German language.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Then, Mr. President, I have no further questions. Thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Witness, were the inmates of the Concentration Camp Dachau bound to secrecy?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No. Of course, if someone was discharged from the camp by the Gestapo - those cases were few and far between, particularly in the case of the Germans, who were then drafted one had to sign a so-called pledge of secrecy.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Could the inmates of the camp, those inside the camp, who worked on farms, et cetera, talk to the other workers about conditions in the camp?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, there were opportunities, because the people worked in the same rooms and factories with other workers - civilian workers. That was the case in the German armament industry, in the fields, and in all factories in Munich and the surroundings.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: If I understood you correctly, you said previously that visitors, people who delivered things, and customers, also had an opportunity of observing these conditions in the camp without difficulty.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes. Many of these people had access everywhere, in the fields as well as in the various factories, and could observe what life was like in these places.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: And what did they see there in the way of atrocities and ill- treatment, and so forth?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe they saw how the people worked, what they looked like and what was produced there. For instance, I can remember one example of what they saw quite well. At that time I was working in the fields. We were pulling a heavy street roller, 16 men, and a group of girls passed who were on an excursion. When they passed, their leader said very loudly, so that we all could hear it, "Look, those people are so lazy that rather than harness up a team of horses they pull it themselves." That was supposed to be a joke.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Witness, when did you first have occasion, after your liberation from the concentration camp, to tell outside people about those horrible atrocities which you related to us today?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I did not understand that; please repeat.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: When did you first have an opportunity, after your discharge or liberation from the concentration camp, of telling an outsider about these horrible atrocities?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Immediately after the liberation. I was at that time, as chief physician of the concentration camp, interrogated by the American investigating corps; and it was to this corps that I told this story for the first time, and I also gave them various proofs diagrams, and the medical records which I had saved from being burnt.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: That prosecutor believed the information you gave without further ado?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Witness, you said that the Defendant Rosenberg was pointed out to you in the Concentration Camp Dachau shortly after you arrived there.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: When was that?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: In the year 1941; first half of 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: First half?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe so, yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Can you perhaps remember the month?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I cannot remember. I arrived in April; I believe it was between April and July or something like that.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: From April to July 1941?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe so.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Was Rosenberg at that time in uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: He was in uniform.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: In what uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe it was an SS uniform.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: SS uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: It was a - I cannot say that very precisely - but he was in uniform.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: All right, you remember prima facie that it was an SS uniform, that is, a black uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, at that time the SS no longer wore the black uniform, because after the beginning of the war they wore field uniforms and other similar uniforms.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Then, you assume it was a gray uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Something like that; whether it was gray or yellow or brown I don't remember any more.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: That is just the point: whether it was gray, brown, or yellow. Was it a field uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I do not know because from 1939 I was in the concentration camp, and I am not at all familiar with the various German uniforms, ranks, and branches of the Army, and so forth.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: But you just said that during the war they changed the uniform.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, the men in the Gestapo also changed theirs. When I was arrested in 1939, all Gestapo personnel wore this black uniform. Then, after the war broke out most of them wore either green or gray uniforms.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: May I ask you again: Did Rosenberg wear a wartime uniform or a peacetime uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe it was a wartime uniform.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Wartime uniform? The Defendant Rosenberg was pointed out to you by another comrade, wasn't he?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: At what distance?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Well, he was just going down the camp street. That was perhaps 30 or 40 degrees.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Thirty or forty metres you mean?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Well, 30 metres; 30 paces I wanted to say, 30 or 40 paces.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: And had you previously seen photographs of Rosenberg? Did you already have an idea of what Rosenberg looked like?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: And when this comrade showed you Rosenberg, was it then necessary for him to say, "This is Rosenberg"? Didn't you recognize him already from having seen him in the photographs which you had previously...

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I cannot remember that. But when he showed him to me I remembered that I knew him already from the various pictures in the newspapers.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: May I ask you to describe the incident precisely? How it happened; where you were standing; where Rosenberg came from; and who was in his company.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Who was in his company? I knew only the camp commander.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Who was the camp commander at that time?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Pierkowski was camp commander, Sturmbannfuehrer Pierkowski.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Do you know whether he is still alive?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, I don't.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: The camp commander?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Pierkowski. Then the Lagerfuehrer Ziel and Hoffmann, I knew them.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Now were you in your room and looking out of the window?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, we were in one of the so-called "block" streets. This led into another street along which the visitors passed.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: And what was said to you?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: "Look, there goes Rosenberg."

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Was Rosenberg alone?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, he was with the other persons.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: That is to say, only with the camp commander?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, there were many other people with 'trim.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: That is to say, he had an escort, a staff?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Members of Rosenberg's staff?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I don't know whether that was Rosenberg's staff, but there were a number of persons.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: A number of persons? Witness, the Defendant Rosenberg assures me most definitely that he has never been to the concentration camp at Dachau. Is it possible that there has been a mistake?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I believe I am not mistaken. Besides the German in question knew Rosenberg very well, I believe.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: How do you know that?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Because he told me so definitely. Otherwise, I have no way of knowing that.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Thoma.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You will forgive me if I point out to you that this is intended to be an expeditious trial and that it is not right to take up too much time upon small points like this.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: My Lord, I ask your permission to remark that the question of whether or not Rosenberg was in the concentration camp is of decisive importance. I thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. OTTO PANNENBECKER (Counsel for Defendant Frick)
turns.text: The Defendant Frick states that he has never been in Dachau Camp. Therefore, in order to clarify the facts I should like to ask the following questions: Witness, at what distance do you believe you saw Frick?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I saw him from the window as he passed with a number of people.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Did you know Frick before?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Yes, from pictures.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: From pictures? Did you recognize him yourself or did some friend tell you that it was Frick?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: A number of us saw him and I looked at him particularly, because at that time he was already Protector of Bohemia and Moravia. For that reason I had a personal interest in recognizing him.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Did Frick wear a uniform?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I do not believe so.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Did you recognize anybody who was with him, anyone from his staff or from the camp command?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I did not know his staff. From the camp command there was Camp Commander Weiter. Camp Commander Weiter, and his adjutant, Otto.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Could you name anyone of your comrades who also recognized him?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: There were many comrades of mine who at that time were standing at the window. Unfortunately, I cannot say who they were, because, as you will understand, life in the concentration camp was so full of incidents that one could not record these things accurately in one's memory. One remembers only the more important events.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Did you recognize him at once of your own accord when he passed by, or had it been mentioned previously that Frick was expected?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, it was not mentioned then. We simply heard that a high-ranking visitor was expected, and we were waiting for this high-ranking visitor. We were not told beforehand who it would be.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: Did you recognize Frick immediately when you came into the courtroom, or did you know beforehand that he was sitting in the fourth seat here?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: No, I recognized him easily, because I have already seen him many times in various pictures, and because he is a well known person in Bohemia and Moravia.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: You believe then that there can be no question of any error.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I don't think so.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: May I then ask the Court whether Frick himself may take the stand to testify that he has never seen Dachau Camp? I want to make this motion now so that, if necessary, the witness might be confronted with Frick.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Counsel for the defendants will understand that they will have the opportunity, when it comes to their time to present their cases, to call all the defendants, but they will not have an opportunity of calling them now. They will have to wait until the case for the Prosecution is over and they will then have an opportunity, each of them, to call the defendant for whom they appear, if they wish to.

turns.speaker: DR. PANNENBECKER
turns.text: I simply thought, that as the witness is available now... [Dr. Kubuschok approached the lectern.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: It is now 5:00 o'clock and unless you are going to be very short. . . are you going to be very short?

turns.speaker: DR. EGON KUBUSCHOK (Counsel for the Reich Cabinet)
turns.text: Yes, Sir. [Turning to the witness.] Witness, you said that when prominent visitors came to the camp, for instance, Reich ministers, extensive preparations were made beforehand. You also said that undesirable persons were removed. Maybe you could supplement that statement. I am interested to know what the purpose of these preparations was.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: I meant that everything had to be in order. In our infirmary all the patients had to lie in bed quietly, everything was washed and prepared; the instruments were polished, as is usually the case for high-ranking visitors. We were not allowed to do anything - no operations; no bandages nor food were given out before the visit had terminated.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Could you perhaps tell me which undesirable persons were to be removed, as you said before?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: Well, the Russians especially were always kept strictly in their blocks. It was said that they were afraid of possible demonstrations, assassinations, et cetera.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Were prisoners kept out of sight because they showed outward signs of ill-treatment?

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: It goes without saying that before the visitors nobody was struck, beaten, hanged, or executed.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: To sum up, the purpose of these preparations was to prevent the guests from seeing the concentration camp as it really was.

turns.speaker: BLAHA
turns.text: From seeing the cruelties.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Court will not sit in open session tomorrow, Saturday, and will only sit in the morning on Monday, because there is work to be done in the closed session tomorrow and on Monday afternoon. I thought it would be convenient for counsel to know that. The Court will now adjourn. [The Tribunal adjourned until 14 January 1946 at 1000 hours.]
